analogy: Think of "Equality" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 3 Equality This chapter is about the concept of equality, a value that is also enshrined in our Constitution. In reflecting on this concept it examines the following questions: o What is equality? Why should we be concerned about this moral and political ideal?... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 3.1 WHY DOES EQUALITY MATTER?, LET’S DO IT D o, FACT SHEET ON GLOBAL INEQUALITIES, ECONOMIC INEQUALITIES IN INDIA. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Equality is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 3 Equality This chapter is about the concept of equality, a value that is also enshrined in our Constitution.

The text explains that in reflecting on this concept it examines the following questions: o What is equality?

The text explains that why should we be concerned about this moral and political ideal? o Does the pursuit of equality involve treating everyone the same way in every condition? o How may we pursue equality and minimise inequality in different spheres of life? o How do we distinguish between different dimensions of equality — political, economic and social?

The text explains that in the course of understanding and answering these questions, you would encounter some important ideologies of our time — socialism, marxism, liberalism and feminism.

The text explains that in this chapter you will see facts and figures about the conditions of inequality.

The text explains that these are only for you to appreciate the nature of inequality; the facts and figures need not be memorised.

The text explains that equality Equality 32 Political Theory 3.1 WHY DOES EQUALITY MATTER?

The text explains that equality is a powerful moral and political ideal that has inspired and guided human society for many centuries.

The text explains that it is implicit in all faiths and religions which proclaim all human beings to be the creation of God.

The text explains that as a political ideal the concept of equality invokes the idea that all human beings have an equal worth regardless of their colour, gender, race, or nationality.

The text explains that it maintains that human beings deserve equal consideration and respect because of their common humanity.

The text explains that it is this notion of a shared humanity that lies behind, for instance, the notions of universal human rights or ‘crimes against humanity’.

The text explains that in the modern period the equality of all human beings has been used as a rallying slogan in the struggles against states and social institutions which uphold inequalities of rank, wealth status or privilege, among people.

The text explains that in the eighteenth century, the French revolutionaries used the slogan ‘Liberty, Equality and Fraternity’ to revolt against the landed feudal aristocracy and the monarchy.

The text explains that the demand for equality was also raised during anti-colonial liberation struggles in Asia and Africa during the twentieth century.

The text explains that it continues to be raised by struggling groups such as Everyone I know believes in a religion.

The text explains that equalityEqualityPolitical Theory 33 women or dalits who feel marginalised in our society.

The text explains that today, equality is a widely accepted ideal which is embodied in the constitutions and laws of many countries.

The text explains that yet, it is inequality rather than equality which is most visible around us in the world as well as within our own society.

The text explains that source: Human Development Report, 2005, UNDP.

The text explains that are they a permanent and inevitable feature of social life which reflects the differences of talent and ability of human beings as well as their different contributions towards social progress and prosperity?

The text explains that these are questions that have troubled people all over the world for many years.

The text explains that it is a question of this kind that makes equality one of the central themes of social and political theory.

The text explains that a student of political theory has to address a range of questions, such as, what does equality imply?

The text explains that some other questions that have been raised regarding Why talk about global or national inequalities when all around us there are inequalities to which nobody seems to object?

The text explains that how should a society decide which differences of treatment or reward are acceptable and which are not?

The text explains that also, what kind of policies should we pursue to try and make the society more egalitarian?

The text explains that no society treats all its members in exactly the same way under all conditions.

The text explains that the smooth functioning of society requires division of work and functions and people often enjoy different status and rewards on account of it.

The text explains that the question that arises is which distinctions and differences are acceptable and which are not?

The text explains that equality of Opportunities The concept of equality implies that all people, as human beings, are entitled to the same rights and opportunities to develop their skills and talents, and to pursue their goals and ambitions.

The text explains that this means that in a society people may differ with regard to their choices and preferences.

The text explains that but just because only some become ace cricketers or successful lawyers, it does not follow that the society should be considered unequal.

The text explains that in other words, it is not the lack of equality of status or wealth or privilege that is significant but the inequalities in people’s access to such basic goods, as education, health care, safe housing, that make for an unequal and unjust society.

The text explains that equalityEqualityPolitical Theory 37 are different from socially-produced inequalities which emerge as a consequence of inequalities of opportunity or the exploitation of some groups in a society by others.

The text explains that social inequalities on the other hand are those created by society.

The text explains that differences of this kind reflect the values of a society and some of these may certainly appear to us to be unjust.

The text explains that this distinction is sometimes useful in helping us to distinguish between acceptable and unfair inequalities in society but it is not always clear or self-evident.

The text explains that therefore, it was felt that denying women equal rights could be justified.

The text explains that for instance, advances in medical science and technologies have helped many disabled people to function effectively in society.

The text explains that given all these complexities, it would be difficult to use the natural/ socially-produced distinction as a standard by which the laws and policies of a society can be assessed.

The text explains that for this reason many theorists today differentiate between inequality arising from our choices and inequalities operating on account of the family or circumstance in which a person is born.

The text explains that it is the latter that is a source of concern to advocates of equality and which they wish to minimise and eliminate.

The text explains that 3.3 THREE DIMENSIONS OF EQUALITY After considering what kind of social differences are unacceptable we need to ask what are the different dimensions of equality that we may pursue or seek to achieve in society.

The text explains that while identifying different kinds of inequalities that exist in society, various thinkers and ideologies have highlighted three main dimensions of equality namely, political, social and economic.

The text explains that it is only by addressing each of these three different dimensions of equality can we move towards a more just and equal society.

The text explains that (a) Every child in class will read the text of the play by turn.

The text explains that (b) The Government of Canada encouraged white Europeans to migrate to Canada from the end of the Second World War till 1960.

The text explains that (c) There is a separate railway reservation counter for the senior citizens.

The text explains that equality Equality 52 Political Theory (d) Access to some forest areas is reserved for certain tribal communities.

The text explains that here are some arguments in favour of the right to vote for women.

The text explains that which of these are consistent with the idea of equality?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 3: Equality

Do not add outside examples to Equality; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 3.1 WHY DOES EQUALITY MATTER? and LET’S DO IT D o to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
